Title: Arabidopsis ACYL CARRIER PROTEIN4 and RHOMBOID LIKE10 act independently in chloroplast phosphatidate synthesis

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: de novo fatty acid biosynthesis and chloroplast glycerolipid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: chloroplast phosphatidic acid/DAG precursor supply branch for galactolipid and phosphatidylglycerol synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: malonyl/acyl intermediates carried on ACP during plastid fatty acid synthesis; acyl-ACP donor pool for GPAT/LPAT-utilizing reactions
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: acyl-ACP pool supporting PA and DAG precursor formation
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Acts as the major plastid acyl carrier/scaffold for de novo fatty acid biosynthesis and supplies acyl-ACP substrates to acyl-ACP-utilizing enzymes for chloroplast phosphatidic acid synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes chloroplast lipid biosynthesis by maintaining acyl-ACP pool size/composition and supporting PA precursor formation for MGDG and PG biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ACP4 is the major leaf ACP isoform and loss of ACP4 caused ~90% reduction in total acyl-ACP content. 2. acp4 mutants showed reduced total chloroplast lipids and altered MGDG, PG, SQDG, and DGDG molecular species, with a strong loss of C16-containing species. 3. ACP4 physically interacted with LPAT and SAD, supporting a direct role in channeling acyl-ACP substrates into chloroplast lipid assembly. 4. ACP4 overexpression did not rescue the rbl10 lipid phenotype, indicating ACP4 and RBL10 act independently.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant analysis (acp4-1, acp4-2); acp4 rbl10 double mutant analysis; ACP4 overexpression in Col-0 and rbl10; acyl-ACP profiling; leaf/chloroplast lipidomics; membrane yeast 2-hybrid
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Most abundant ACP isoform in Arabidopsis leaves
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Most abundant ACP isoform in leaves
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Normal growth under 16 h light/8 h dark at 22/18 °C; acetate [14C] feeding assays in isolated chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point not formally established; major determinant of acyl-ACP precursor pool size/composition
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: LC-MS/MS lipidomics; acyl-ACP LC-MS/MS; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to tune chloroplast membrane lipid composition and C16/C18 partitioning, but manipulation may cause pale-green phenotype, reduced chlorophyll, and lower total lipid content; not sufficient to overcome RBL10-related defects alone
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Pale green, smaller plants, >2-fold lower chlorophyll, reduced total photosynthetic membrane lipids
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ACP4 disruption specifically reduced plastid C16 fatty acid contribution and shifted lipid balance toward ER-derived species; overexpression increased C18:3 and reduced C16:3 in MGDG/PG

Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: chloroplast phosphatidic acid to diacylglycerol conversion linked to galactolipid biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: chloroplast PA-to-DAG branch for MGDG biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: 34:x diacylglycerol precursor pool for MGDG biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Appears to promote turnover/conversion of chloroplast-derived 34:x PA into DAG used for MGDG biosynthesis; direct biochemical activity remains unresolved
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes chloroplast-pathway MGDG biosynthesis by supporting PA turnover to DAG; loss causes PA accumulation, reduced 34:x DAG, and shift toward ER-derived galactolipid species
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. rbl10 mutants accumulated 34:x PA and showed decreased 34:x DAG and C16:3-containing MGDG, indicating impaired PA-to-DAG turnover. 2. Isolated rbl10 chloroplasts displayed slower [14C]PA turnover and delayed flux from PA into MGDG. 3. RBL10 disruption shifted MGDG and DGDG toward ER-derived 36:x species. 4. The PA-turnover defect was partially compensated in the acp4 rbl10 double mutant, indicating independent but interacting metabolic effects.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant analysis; acp4 rbl10 double mutant analysis; leaf/chloroplast lipidomics; isolated chloroplast acetate [14C] feeding assays
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Normal growth under 16 h light/8 h dark at 22/18 °C; acetate [14C] feeding assays in isolated chloroplasts
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for chloroplast PA turnover to DAG/MGDG suggested, but not formally established as rate-limiting
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Candidate for engineering chloroplast galactolipid composition and plastid/ER lipid flux partitioning, but direct manipulation may alter photosynthetic membrane composition and MGDG/PG balance
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Altered chloroplast lipid composition, especially reduced C16:3-MGDG and lower MGDG/PG ratio
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct biochemical activity remains elusive; authors suggest roles in PA turnover, PA flipping across inner envelope membrane, or related mechanisms rather than confirmed proteolysis
